data.title: Nebius: The Q4 Results Should Trigger A Rally (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
My Coverage History And Thesis Update On Nebius

I'm glad to be one of the first analysts on Seeking Alpha who laid out a bullish thesis on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) when the stock price was trading at $36/share and then dropped to ~$22/share in April 2025. I trimmed my large position close to $50-52/share after the first doubling, as I was heavily buying the massive dip at low-20s, getting overexposed to NBIS at some point. The trimming was stress-relieving, but it's almost physically painful to imagine the opportunity cost that I incurred along with it.

Anyway, I want to move on. My past three articles on NBIS compared the firm with CoreWeave (CRWV) and IREN (IREN), and the latest two articles were more bullish on NBIS than on any of its peers. Today, my preview article is going to be dedicated mainly to the upcoming Q4 earnings on February 13. The company is reporting in less than 3 weeks, and I see no articles discussing about Nebius Group's Q4 results specifically, so let me pioneer this topic of the season.

From what I see, the current consensus expectations are still pricing slightly more conservatively than what management guided for, because the firm has clearly been just a vision-driven "belief" story for investors. And the revenue beating track record isn't the one any public company would envy - the difficulty in properly setting the revenue targets based on the guided ARRs took its toll.

Still, I think Nebius is getting closer to its execution-driven model, where delivery speed dictates revenues. I think the management will give us some updates on Nebius' Finnish data center phases, which were scheduled for December, and the launch of the New Jersey and London facilities. Any miss on the top or bottom line shouldn't matter that much, in my view - it's about operational updates that should drive the stock's price action. Since I have a high conviction in the management team and their ability to move forward quickly, I think the Q4 updates will be bullish, and they'll be able to push the stock price back to its previous local highs. I keep my Buy in place ahead of the Q4 print.

Previewing Nebius Group's Q4 Earnings

In Nebius' Q3 earnings update, the management team decided to tighten the FY2025 revenue guidance to $500-550 million so that the midpoint becomes "clearer," so to speak. In other words, the full-year revenue midpoint is now at $525 million, while the previous guidance was pointing at $450-630 million with the midpoint at $540 million. A narrower range was actually a good thing, in my opinion, because it gave us much more clarity. But the market punished NBIS with a 7%+ sell-off right after the print, and NBIS stayed under some pressure throughout the whole calendar Q4.

The intentional cutting of the higher end of the initial guidance was a smart move, in my opinion, as it freed up some room for possible miss. Now, if NBIS delivers $540 million like it was forecasted initially, that would be a beat of ~2.8% to the guidance midpoint. They don't need to deliver close to $690 million to beat. However, the problem with the management's midpoint guidance this time is that the market is expecting NBIS to reach a slightly higher revenue in Q4 versus the updated high-end prediction:

I think they're likely to beat that high-end anyway. Why?

Throughout the previous earnings call, the management noted that the core infrastructure's revenue was capped by the capacity limits and timing of additional capacity going online, not the demand environment. If so, then the upcoming Q4 data will be essential because we'll have updates on NBIS's success in Finland and New Jersey (plus the London facility, which was added in November).

And we are growing with the numbers of the regions where we are present. And we're also bringing new capacity online in the current sites. We're also coming online in New Jersey. We are launching new phases of Finland in Q4, which are also presold by the way.

Source: NBIS's Q3 earnings call, Andrey Korolenko's comments

I think the facility they launched in London in early November will drive up their revenues to an extent that analysts failed to account for. Why? Well, management didn't mention it on the earnings call, and analysts didn't bother to ask questions about that specific facility, so I assume it's a net addition to the revenue side in Q4.

I saw some commentary on X and elsewhere, with some analysts expecting Microsoft's (MSFT) deal and the contract with Meta (META) to influence the upcoming Q4 revenue in a positive way. But it's misleading to me because both deals are back-weighted, so NBIS will likely see its revenue surge in late 2026. However, I don't rule out the significance of these deals when it comes to driving the narrative. We saw how new deals additions have impacted the management's views before - see the Q3 earnings call and the CEO's words on regarding the ARR long-term trends.

And we have a large pipeline of new software and services that we are continuing to build, which will differentiate us from other cloud companies. Based on the strength in demand that we see and our accelerated capacity growth plan, we believe we can achieve annualized run rate revenue ARR of $7 billion to $9 billion by the end of 2026.

So, if the management comes out with yet another update regarding the volume rise or ARR boost, the stock price will likely see a massively positive reaction right after such commentaries.

NBIS: Valuation Update

I recall that when I called for trimming NBIS in June 2025, its FY2026 P/S ratio was slightly over 8x. Now it's less than 6.7x, with a forecasted slump below 3x by the end of 2027, based on the updated consensus estimates (YCharts data below).

There's also one interesting thing: Two Wall Street analysts who form the consensus for FY2027 predict an EPS range of -$1.28 to $6.00, with a midpoint of $2.36, based on Seeking Alpha. But then, for FY2028, we see a complete absence of diversity of opinion - there's only one analyst forming the consensus.

It tells me that NBIS has a massive opportunity for repricing in terms of its EPS because a) the management seeks to run operations profitable (we remember that from the CEO's commentary regarding EBITDA breakeven), and b) as the new capacity finally kicks in and starts to generate revenues, and more analysts assess that, the consensus will naturally move to the upside.

With all that in mind, I think the fair price-to-sales multiple for NBIS - if it breaks even on the adjusted EBITDA front and scales profitably on that metric - should be close to 6-8x by the end of 2027. With that in mind, the implied upside potential, conservatively assuming no changes to the current FY2027 sales consensus, arrives at a $46.68 billion market cap. The stock might double over the next 1-2 years if it follows the above path I've just described.

The Bottom Line

The key risk to my bullish update lies in NBIS's execution and the management's ability to deliver on time. There are a lot of moving parts in the neocloud sector, and everything that makes it run, with plenty of partners, suppliers, and market conditions that Nebius Group can't control. Another risk is that in the case of another miss, NBIS stock could drop heavily, as was the case after Q4 2024 or Q3 2025. Plus, I noticed that many ordinary investors have become interested in this neocloud name, and if they overreact to a miss, they might decide to bail out and create a massive supply of NBIS stock, thus pressuring the price.

Despite all that, I believe the upcoming quarterly release and subsequent updates from the management team might spark another rally if execution at the Finnish, New Jersey, and London facilities goes as planned (or even ahead of schedule). I haven't covered non-core business lines today because they're relatively small and don't contribute much to NBIS's neocloud story. But who knows, the non-core assets can surprise us to the upside as well. At least I saw that Clickhouse raised a new financing round at a higher valuation mark. We shall see.

For now, I think NBIS should have a Buy rating. Let me know what you think of NBIS stock ahead of its Q4 earnings release in the comment section below.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: IREN: Why I'm Still Bullish Heading Into Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While I've been bullish on IREN Limited (IREN) from the very start, this is the first time we're seeing a shift in the market’s engagement with the story. As the stock is up 16% since my last coverage, it is no longer valued on the narrative or the AI optionality, it is valued as a forward-deployed infrastructure play where execution is key.

Note that I am not anchoring this thesis on reported revenues but rather on contracted ARR that converts to its underlying form. The economics are already visible via the dated contracts, including the Microsoft (MSFT) one, which implies significant annualized revenues when power is activated. The issue isn’t demand, it’s the sequencing of power availability.

This call isn’t about beating revenues or EPS. It’s a confirmation call to ensure contracted ARR continues to convert from reserved to active. If the conversion timelines hold up, the income statement is simply lagging the truth, and the thesis remains firmly intact.

ARR First, Revenue Later: Why the Income Statement Is Lagging Reality

If we consider the case of IREN, we don’t anchor based on revenue figures that they report, but rather anchor based on the emerging ARR, which is actually reflected in the Microsoft contract that implies up to approximately $1.9 billion in annualized revenue potential once fully phased in and power is activated.

And this is where investors might mistakenly interpret the income statement. ARR is increasing faster than it is recognized. The ramp is controlled by power availability and deployment sequence, rather than by demand. Thus, revenue is recognized after the underlying economic reality is built beneath the surface.

What I am focusing on in the coming call is not the headline ARR growth, but rather the degree to which contracted ARR is turning into its underlying form. The level of reserved capacity, the level of capacity that’s scheduled, the level of capacity that’s already monetizable, these numbers matter much more than the quarter-to-quarter growth in revenues. If the company can make the case that the ARR is essentially locked in, it’s just waiting on the power to be turned on, the market will continue to ignore the noise.

Connected Power vs. Active Power: What is the Real KPI?

One of the biggest distinctions in this story, and what I think sets sophisticated investors from casual ones, is the distinction between connected power and active power. IREN was very transparent in terms of the fact that it had control over a large power portfolio. However, control alone is not what creates revenue. Revenue is created only when the megawatts are actually plugged in, or energized, or connected.

Currently, a large chunk of IREN’s strength is either under construction or awaiting energization. The company’s Sweetwater 1 alone represents ~1.4GW in gross load that is either under construction or awaiting energization and is still guided for April 2026. This earnings call is at a critical juncture at which investors are looking to be reassured that the timelines remain in place and the construction risks are well managed.

New megawatts? No thanks. I just need the management to reinforce the message that the existing megawatts on the pipeline continue to move from connected to active as planned. Any evidence of delays/slowdowns on the grid will disproportionately impact the Street as it directly impacts the achievement of the ARR.

However, should management be able to strengthen its assertion of a connected power gradually transitioning to active, revenue-producing power, then it is safe to say that such a thesis stands on firm ground. Power conversion is a bottleneck, demand is not.

Earnings Preview: When Valuation Prices the Back Half

Heading into Q2 FY26, consensus sits at a level of $227.33 million in revenues, along with a loss of -$0.09 EPS, which will be reported on Feb 5, after market. On the surface, it appears to be a lackluster quarter for a company whose stock is currently trading for $56.68, particularly given a D+ grade for its valuation and a forward P/S ratio of 16.21x its FY26 revenues. But, of course, it also does not address what those numbers really mean.

First, revenue growth is not slowing. While the revenue is only $227 million, consensus growth is approximately 90% YoY, which is followed by $280.9 million in Q3, or 89.7% YoY, and $390.9 million in Q4, or 107% YoY. It is not reasonable to expect slowing growth like this and interpret it as demand fatigue as it looks more like capacity gating. Consensus is modeling a big step-up in the back half of the year, and it only makes sense if the power is coming on schedule.

But that’s EPS, where investors get confused. EPS is estimated at $1.05 for FY26, which works out to a forward 53.9x GAAP P/E, which improves marginally to 53.5x in FY27, even though the topline increases by $1.59 billion, or 138% ($1.15 billion to $2.74 billion). The market is not pricing near-term operating leverage, only asset activation followed by margin improvement, as depreciation, SBC, and pre-revenue costs remain front-loaded.

Priced for Success, Tested on Execution

The risk isn’t demand. The risk is execution concentration. There’s a lot of future ARR associated with a small number of hyperscale customers, all with a very sequenced rollout plan. There’s a fragile equilibrium where everything’s working as long as the timelines work.

If the energization process slips or if deployments are delayed or if capital costs escalate unexpectedly, the market’s reaction is almost instant. The market doesn’t wait to be told why something has not worked out. The market de-risks first and then re-evaluates later. This is the cost of being priced-for-success.

This is why I think the upcoming earnings is really a trust check. Investors aren’t asking the management team to prove the vision again. They’re actually asking the management team to prove that the machine is still working exactly as it was last quarter.

Takeaway

However, the upcoming earnings is not about IREN beating or missing the revenue and/or EPS estimates. It is a confirmation call on the execution side. Investors would be looking to be reassured on the progress and confirmation on the power activation, sequencing, and construction timetables, especially on the Sweetwater side.

data.title: Energy Transfer And Capital Spending Shifts: Implications For MLP Cash Flows
data.description:
Midstream investors are once again paying close attention to capital budgets. After several years of prioritizing payouts and balance sheet repair, large pipeline operators are beginning to diverge in how aggressively they reinvest. Some are leaning into new projects, while others are deliberately pulling back. For master limited partnerships, this distinction matters. Capital spending directly influences distributable cash flow, coverage ratios, and ultimately the sustainability of distributions.¹

Across the sector, valuations remain well below historical norms despite relatively stable operating fundamentals.² That disconnect has renewed focus on how individual partnerships balance growth ambitions with cash discipline.

Energy Transfer’s Pipeline Push

Energy Transfer (ET) entered 2026 signaling a renewed emphasis on growth. Management announced plans to allocate roughly $5 to $5.5 billion toward expansion projects, with most of the spending directed toward natural gas infrastructure.³ The strategy reflects expectations for rising demand tied to power generation, data centers, and industrial activity.

To support this shift, the partnership paused development of its proposed Lake Charles LNG export terminal while accelerating investment in pipeline and processing assets that can be placed into service more quickly.⁴ Several of these projects are expected to begin contributing to cash flows during 2026, supporting management’s expectation of improving earnings as volumes ramp.⁵

In the near term, however, higher capital outlays absorb cash that might otherwise support distributions or faster debt reduction. Energy Transfer has emphasized that it intends to fund projects internally, but elevated spending still places pressure on coverage if timelines slip or costs rise.⁶ Investors remain mindful of prior cycles when aggressive capital programs strained cash flows, reinforcing the importance of disciplined execution.

Diverging Paths Across the Midstream Sector

Not all large MLPs are taking the same approach. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is moving toward the end of a multiyear expansion cycle. After several years of elevated spending, management expects capital expenditures to decline meaningfully in 2026, freeing up excess cash that can be allocated to distributions, unit repurchases, or balance sheet improvements.⁷

Kinder Morgan (KMI) is pursuing a similarly measured path, with capital plans focused primarily on internally funded expansions rather than large, balance sheet-intensive projects.⁸

For investors, these diverging strategies highlight an important trade-off. Companies increasing capital spending may offer stronger long-term growth, but often at the expense of near-term cash flexibility. Firms scaling back investment tend to generate steadier free cash flow, which can support distribution stability even if growth moderates.

What It Means for Cash Flows and Valuations

Despite solid fundamentals, many MLPs continue to trade below historical valuation ranges.² Capital allocation decisions help explain why. Elevated spending can delay the realization of cash returns, even when underlying assets perform well. Conversely, restrained budgets can quickly improve coverage metrics and reinforce investor confidence.

Capital spending is not merely an accounting detail. It is the primary lever that determines how much cash ultimately reaches unitholders. Investors evaluating midstream partnerships should weigh not just headline income but also where each company sits in its investment cycle. The most compelling opportunities often emerge when disciplined capital plans align with durable, contract-backed cash flows.

Footnotes

  1. Reuters, “Energy Transfer expects to spend up to $5.5 billion in 2026 on natgas network,” January 6, 2026.

  2. Geoffrey Seiler, “Energy Transfer vs. Enterprise Products Partners: Which High-Yield Pipeline Stock Will Outperform in 2026?” Nasdaq, December 14, 2025.

  3. Reuters, “Energy Transfer expects to spend up to $5.5 billion in 2026 on natgas network,” January 6, 2026.

  4. Matt DiLallo, “Every Energy Transfer Investor Should Keep an Eye on This Number,” The Motley Fool, June 13, 2025.

  5. “Energy Transfer Expects to Stomp on the Gas in 2026,” Nasdaq, December 18, 2025.

  6. Matt DiLallo, “Every Energy Transfer Investor Should Keep an Eye on This Number,” Nasdaq, June 13, 2025.

  7. Matt DiLallo, “The First Energy Stock I Plan to Buy in 2026,” The Motley Fool, January 4, 2026.

  8. Kinder Morgan, “Kinder Morgan Announces 2026 Financial Expectations,” Business Wire, December 8, 2025.

data.title: Long Cast Advisers Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners & Friends:

For the 4Q25 quarter (ended December 31, 2025), cumulative net returns improved 1%. For the year, our cumulative portfolio was flat. Since inception in November 2015 through quarter end, LCA has returned a cumulative 271% net of fees, or 14% CAGR. Despite this year’s underperformance, since inception, we comfortably exceed three widely used representative indices for passive small company investing: The Russell 2000 Index, the iShares US MicroCap ETF and the iShares SmallCap EAFE (ex-N. Am) ETF. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Individual account returns may vary. 1

With a concentrated portfolio of small businesses that we intend to own for multiple years, it should be expected that our returns will be different from the overall market, as they were this year and have been over our 10-years of business. At year end, Long Cast managed 28 SMA accounts with AUM of $12M, still small but growing. Account sizes range from $25K to $3M (smaller accounts managed for existing clients) with an average account size of $430K. As we grow, we accumulate resources to do deeper and better research. That is exciting.

Long Cast was founded to provide long-term and patient investing in well-researched and well- understood small- and micro-cap companies that can grow over time. It aims for 15% annualized returns (aka “five year doubles”). Long Cast operates as an alternative to the passive investing, and with more transparency and less expense than a fund. I occasionally consider converting to a fund but always conclude that greatest beneficiary of such a move would be me, a notion that violates the principle that business exists to solve problems for its customers.

Speaking of Long Cast customers, they include individuals and institutions who are given the choice of paying an AUM fee or a performance fee (“qualified clients” only). Though I “leave money on the table” in good years, given my preference for long term and capital efficient investing, a compensation structure that rewards tax inefficient short-term trading would be incongruent. Furthermore, since all of my investable net worth is invested alongside my customers’, if I am a good investor, which is to say, good at analyzing businesses and owning them at the right concentration, and as importantly, good at avoiding the bad ones at any concentration, we will all do well over time regardless of fee structure.

Portfolio Update

In 4Q25 MAMA, PESI and PDEX were the biggest contributors to returns while RSSS and MTRX were the largest detractors. At year end, the top five positions represented 62% of the portfolio. For the full year, MAMA, CCRD and PESI led returns, offset by declines in QRHC, RSSS and the unfortunate permanent loss of capital in our small position in LSYN.

Our largest purchases in the quarter were PDEX, CCRN and NRC. A summary of our thinking on why we acquired these follows. For the year, our largest purchases were PDEX, PESI and NRC and our largest sales were CCRD and RELL. As you may recall, CCRD was acquired in October and is no longer listed. Cumulative gains on the stock at year-end exceeded $1M. In 2025 we realized ~60% of these gains (mostly long-term) and rolled the remainder into its acquirer, Euronet Worldwide (EEFT). There is a bear market for legacy payments stalwarts like EEFT, but it is profitable and cash generative, trades for a single digit valuation and has several potential growth tailwinds including the benefits from the CCRD purchase that may reward investors over time.

PDEX ($28.51 avg price). We discussed PDEX at length in our 3Q25 letter.

CCRN ($8.24 avg price). We are revisiting the nurse staffing company we first owned back in 2019 and sold when COVID comps turned negative. At $8 / share and with 32M shares out, the company has a $260M market capitalization. At its most recent quarter, the company had $99M in net cash and no debt. In December it received a $20M termination payment following its failed $615M acquisition by AYA. This leaves it with over 40% of its market cap in cash and a pro forma enterprise value of $140M.

Of the large nurse staffing companies, CCRN is one of the smallest and has long lagged its peer group in profitability. We originally owned this because founder Kevin Clark had returned after a +20-year absence, and the stock was cheap. We had anticipated that his management and entrepreneurial experience would alter the culture and cadence of the business, and help it achieve margins closer to industry averages.

The COVID boom certainly changed the cadence of the business, enabling the company to pay down debt, make sound acquisitions, buy back stock and accumulate cash. It also saw a change in management: In 2021, Clark hired John Martins from privately held AYA Healthcare, one of the largest nurse staffing companies, to run the nurse staffing division at CCRN. Martins was promoted to CEO in 2022 and Clark remained Chairman.

In late 2024, AYA agreed to purchase CCRN for $615M with the intent on running it as a separate and independent division and brand. However, the merger collapsed in late 2025 after the deal didn’t close in a timely fashion, delayed by FTC reviews (compounded by the government shutdown), and further frustrated by the unexpected death of AYA’s founder, who had initially negotiated the deal. After the merger’s demise a few weeks before the new year, Martins departed and Clark returned as CEO.

Thus, the investment case is a bit similar to 2019, but today it is cheaper, with a stronger balance sheet, and due to businesses acquired during COVID, with a stronger presence in higher margin niches of the healthcare staffing business including locum tenens (physician staffing), home health care and education staffing (ie school nurses). These niches are important because nurse staffing is a highly competitive market, even more so given new entrants attracted to easy money during COVID, and legacy players who are over-levered and need volume to cover debt costs. These factors contribute to continued industry wide pressure on pricing, which still has not returned to pre-COVID levels, and may continue to fall until these issues “wash out”.

I think CCRN is nearing positive comps and we are investing ahead of this turn, with a margin of safety offered by a low multiple, optionality offered by excess cash, and the possibility that Clark’s third go- round can sustainably improve margins. I think it has the potential of achieving our hurdle rate.

NRC ($14.45 avg price). Among our meaningful contributors in the quarter, NRC is our newest position. With 23M shares out and a current market price of $19, the company has a $430M market capitalization and including net debt, a roughly $500M enterprise value. At that price, it trades at a 13x multiple against $40M of annualized EBITDA, using the most recent quarter’s results of $10M, which I believe to be the trough.

The company is broadly a consulting service for hospitals and healthcare administrations and is especially known for being a top-three provider of medical patient surveys and outcome assessment, a business that exists in the overlap between regulatory requirements (CAHPS), entrenched bureaucrats (hospital administrators) and medical marketing. I’ll be honest, it’s not a business I feel great about – it seems like a tax on financially burdened and poorly managed healthcare systems – but as a cousin cardiologist offered, “I hate those patient surveys, but the hospital administrators love them. I don’t know anything about investing but this sounds like a phenomenal business.” Her comment is the least meaningful part of our investment case.

A larger part is that it is an owner-operator company, and those have a history of outperformance. The company was founded in 1981 by Michael Hays, presently Chairman, CEO for most of its history and the largest shareholder through a series of trusts. He had previously worked at Selection Research Inc, also based in Lincoln, Nebraska. SRI is an “OG” market research, psychological testing and employee assessment firm founded in the 1970’s mostly famous for owning Gallup, which it acquired in 1988.

There are many heroic notions of the entrepreneurial and shareholder aligned owner-operator – John Malone, Kjell Rokke and Steve Jobs come to mind – and reading Hays’ shareholder letters going back to 2003, he seems to fancy himself along those lines. Financial results over the years offers more accurate testimony, which brings us to a critical benefit of owner-operators; if their stye of management does not lead to sustained success, then the pain they encounter as owners can nudge them to willingly professionalize the operator. I believe we are investing at this phase of that cycle.

In early 2024, the company made wholesale management changes, adding four C-level positions. It came across my radar around that time, sifting through profitable and high margin companies whose stocks had underperformed and this one seemed exceptional in the consistency of its +30% EBITDA margins and FCF generation. Revenues however were declining, and the stock price followed and it was hard to get a sense if operations were in a temporary trough or in secular decline, the critical difference between an investment opportunity and a value trap.

At the time, the management changes appeared (to me) more like a “spin around” than a turnaround, and indeed, the new hires did not stick. But a little more than year later, in March 2025, the company announced the hiring of a new CEO, Trent Green from Amazon One. This signaled a more serious approach and is a significant catalyst for owning the stock.

Another catalyst was the October 2025, $6.75B acquisition of Press Ganey, owned by Ares Capital, by Qualtrics, owned by Silver Lake. (Ares had acquired Press Ganey from EQT in 2019, reportedly for $4B.) The PE-backed deal between two of the leading companies in the space potentially creates risk of a competitive behemoth, but it concurrently affirms that this industry can generate cash, or else PE isn’t likely involved. Furthermore, the size of the deal indicates the industry is big enough to support growth of our $140M revenue company. Finally, the rumored revenue multiple on the deal (appended with a grain of salt) would value our company in the billions. Though Silver Lake is said to be an elite PE firm, the deal also raises the potential for customer disruptions and the possibility that short term financial decisions are prioritized over long term operating decisions, a trade-off where we happily take the other side.

Dramatically improving deferred revenue is the most important catalyst for owning the stock. Deferred revenue reflects a future obligation to a customer, and therefore is a balance sheet liability. Sometimes and in some industries this liability is accompanied with cash, which in certain cases acts as a kind of float. Not all deferred revenue includes cash and not all cash deferred revenues qualifies as float, but deferred revenue nearly always signifies a future sale, because that sale satisfies the obligation. The decade long chart showing NRC sales vs deferred revenues seems to signal a positive turn in revenues is near.

There’s a write up of NRC on VIC (for subscribers) that covers additional aspects of the business, but I think the financial highlights speak for themselves, notably the consistent profitability, high margins and the lack of shareholder dilution. Throughout my research – and this hasn’t been easy to conduct since the company has suspended investor outreach and everything on its website is consultant jargon - I have questioned the durability of these profits in an AI centered world of thin-walleted hospitals. This is why I value the PE-deal as a strong signal. And despite my bias against consultant speak, according to a friend who sits on a local hospital board, NRC’s services are highly valued by hospital customers. It might not make sense to me why a patient-survey- healthcare-consultancy generates 30% EBITDA margins, but I can’t let my cognitive biases disadvantage our potential returns given so much other supportive data, (thus applying the expensive lesson from when I passed on XPEL because “who needs a car condom?”)

Among our other top holdings:

MTRX ($7.34 avg price). The bear case against MTRX, one of our largest positions, is that despite its historically high backlog, it can’t build profitably. And in fact, recent financial results support this view. As discussed in prior letters, one would have to go back to pre-COVID times to understand the earnings power in the business. I believe we are near the inflection where this should become clear. As I wrote in the 3Q25 letter, if they can show profitability, which should come as soon as next quarter, I believe doubters will have to reconsider their thinking and “re-rate” the multiple, now less than 8x a “bad scenario” $20M in EBITDA (as a reminder, there’s $16M of EBITDA just from adding back D&A plus stock based comp). Furthermore, while acknowledging so much uncertainty in our foreign affairs, if the Gulf Coast refineries resume processing heavy Venezuelan crude (as they’re optimized for), it could have secondary and tertiary benefits to MTRX.

RSSS ($2.57 avg price). The company sells a subscription product on a per seat basis to an industry that faces contraction as a result of the US federal government withdrawing funds from research and science. My divergent view is that I think it can gain share in a shrinking market, while the stock market prices this as if it can’t.

PESI ($8.15 avg price). Given the kinds of cash flows inferred by the volumes expected at Hanford, why shouldn’t this be worth +$50? This is really just getting started.

In Conclusion: A Case For Empathy

There is a change in this country unlike anything I’ve seen in my lifetime. The anecdotal experience of “my lifetime” is of course meaningless; one can observe episodes of social distress everywhere in the world and throughout history, often compounded by new forms of media. These repetitions are costly. What if we didn’t have to “experience things for ourselves”? Couldn’t we move beyond spasms of war and violence every three or four generations? Lack of empathy – a most overlooked virtue - is a mispriced externality with significant societal costs. Without it, our lizard brains are perpetually trying to confirm priors while blaming “others” for any incongruities. There are alternate paths.

As always, I remain committed to building a durable and sustainable business based on a repeatable investment process and intelligent capital allocation. I remain grateful to have clients (by design) aligned with my long term, small company centric and research-intensive focus. I welcome the continued interest from individuals and institutions as I patiently grow the business.

Sincerely

Avi

Brooklyn, NY

Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Individual account performance may vary. Any investment entails a risk of loss including the total loss of capital.
