data.title: ULTY: Doubtful That The Strategy Change Will Reduce NAV Erosion (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (ULTY) remains extremely popular amongst income investors due to its high dividend yield and its diverse portfolio of holdings. When I previously covered ULTY, I issued a buy rating due to the strategy change and management's attempt at implementing a way to hedge against market declines. Since then, the fund's share price has declined by more than 33% and even when including all distributions paid, ULTY still sits at a total loss of ~11%. Since my last coverage, management has yet again implemented another strategy change that overhauls the fund's approach.

On December 8th, 2025, ULTY now aims to get away from the specific focus on stocks that have a high implied volatility. Instead, ULTY now aims to take a more balanced approach that aligns with better price stability over a longer holding period. So any performance graphs that are dated before December 8th will not reflect the new strategy. Looking at the performance over the last year, we can see that ULTY's share price has eroded by 57.6%. Even when including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of 2.8% over the same time frame.

Despite the shifts, ULTY continues to offer investors a massive starting dividend yield of ~70% and issues payouts on a weekly basis. Since new payouts are declared every week, the annualized yield will likely be different based on the time you read this article. There are some pros and cons of such a large yield, but I wanted to revisit ULTY to specifically focus on the strategy change and my estimated outlook for the fund. It is becoming harder to support the fund's poor performance and while there may still be some utility left, I believe it ultimately comes down to when an investor initiated their position. Investors that have maintained a position for a longer period of time are likely to be less affected by the continuous NAV erosion.

Strategy Shift

The strategy shift was initiated to directly address the concerns around the fund's NAV erosion. YieldMax released an official document that highlighted these changes. After reviewing this update, I wanted to call out what I believe to be some of the most notable changes. There are three primary changes that were initiated, with the most important one being the way that ULTY approaches its holdings. Instead of focusing on high volatility holdings that can generate massive option premiums, the fund now chooses to invest up to 50% of its assets into lower volatility, large-cap equities that can provide stability.

When looking at the top ten holdings of ULTY, we can now see the inclusion of many recognizable large-cap names. Quanta Services (PWR) is the largest holding at 5.71% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other high profile names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG), Lam Research (LRCX), and VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). So not only is ULTY now holding large-cap names, but it also includes exposure to metals like miners for Gold and Silver.

The second most impactful portfolio shift is the inclusion of leveraged option strategies. The biggest downfall of many YieldMax funds is the severely capped upside growth from sold call options. When markets rallied higher throughout 2025, many YieldMax funds lagged behind. So if funds like ULTY failed to capture the upside through one of the strongest bull markets in the last decade, this painted a picture of the fund's underlying inability to be an attractive long-term holding.

To remedy this issue, the fund now uses put spread strategies against the equities that it holds. So since the spreads are being captured on assets that the fund actually holds with the inclusion of leverage, this allows ULTY to earn premiums while the leverage helps it see higher upside. However, I suspect that this leverage will also exacerbate the downside risks as well. So I am less confident in this specific shift over a longer holding period.

Lastly, ULTY also added a new management team to replace two of the existing portfolio managers. The new managers are Quinn Berry and Scott Snyder. This could be a potential risk for the fund but it also represents a fresh perspective on the underlying approach to its hedging strategy. ULTY outlined these hedging techniques in the document, highlighted below:

The Fund may buy a put option on an underlying reference instrument owned by the Fund (a protective put) as a hedging technique in an attempt to protect against an anticipated decline in the market value of the underlying reference instrument. Such hedge protection is provided only during the life of the put option when the Fund, as the buyer of the put option, is able to sell the underlying reference instrument at the put exercise price, regardless of any decline in the underlying instrument’s market price. The Fund may also seek to offset a decline in the value of the underlying reference instrument through appreciation in the value of the put option.

Performance Risks & Outlook

The performance window since these portfolio shifts were initiated is small, so I won't spend too much time on ULTY's performance over the last month. However, a quick glance at the total return, including all distributions paid, shows us that ULTY has a higher total return than the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), since December 8th. However, the share price has suffered from a decline of more than 7.2% over this short operating window. Therefore, it's likely that investors should still anticipate that nearly 100% of their returns with ULTY will come from the fund's distributions over time. However, this short operating window already fails to impress me in terms of the new strategy that's supposed to limit downside risk.

Therefore, I thought it would be helpful to share some scenarios on how ULTY is likely to perform based on different market cycles. Directly referencing the strategies laid out by management, I believe that ULTY's performance can potentially outperformthe prior version of the fund, but it will still underperform traditional indices over a long period of time. Ultimately, the best scenario for the fund would be to see markets rally higher over the course of 2026:

Bull Market

  • ULTY would likely outperform its predecessor because the new leveraged put strategy allows for greater upside potential. Additionally, ULTY now has exposure to Mag 7 holdings and other stable large-cap holdings, so the movements may more closely match the indices. The issue is that the performance may still lag behind the overall market but this is expected. Lastly, this is the best environment for distributions since higher option premiums can lead to higher payouts for shareholders.

Bear Market

  • Since the new strategy enables management to utilize protective puts, ULTY may see lower downside movements. Since a portion of its assets are also allocated to positions meant to stabilize the portfolio, the assumption is that this will also soften the downside risks. This makes sense since ULTY now limits its exposure to high volatility names. However, we have yet to see how this is executed over time.
  • My main issue here is that the old strategy used to generate option premiums that could help buffer downside movements. This new strategy aims to limit the downside risk but the buffer of option premiums is now gone. If this new approach isn't managed carefully, or if management is incorrect with their positioning, the downside risks can actually be amplified since leverage is now involved.

Flat Markets

  • The new strategy may actually be worse for ULTY during flat markets because of the costs associated with the protective puts. Now that the fund is using a selected pool of stocks to stabilize its share price, this means less option premiums. Subtract the lower income by the cost of the protective puts and we get lower returns. However, this isn't necessarily a big deal if the payouts are adjusted downward from their prior highs, which it seems like is the case.

So although I am bullish on the expansion of the market over the course of 2026, I remain cautious about ULTY's new approach if positive market momentum slows. I believe that the large-cap leaders will figure out how to utilize AI to improve operating margins by expanding on its internal efficiencies, such as cutting costs. However, the technology sector can quickly change trajectory just from investor sentiment alone. If earnings growth slows or large capex spending continues to be a point of contention, ULTY's appeal may be limited.

Furthermore, the biggest impact from this strategy is that investors must face the fact that the days of the massive 100% dividend yields are gone. ULTY isn't able to generate a yield of this level since it no longer focuses on holdings with large levels of implied volatility. I believe that a more sustainable yield would be somewhere around the 40% range, which would still be an impressive yield but produce much less NAV erosion over time.

Dividend May Decline Further

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.5054 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 70%. One of the largest issues that I've experienced with ULTY is the shrinking dividend yield over time. Following the prior stock split and continuous reduction in its payouts, long-term holders have seen their annual dividend income significantly decline. This is one of the structural flaws of the fund and means that ULTY has essentially provided the earliest investors with the best income potential.

As the underlying NAV declines, the distributions have to be adjusted downward to offset the lower base of assets. As the fund's net assets decline, ULTY has fewer assets actively generating its income. This is a vicious cycle that will continue unless the market continues to trend higher without interruption. So investors that want to keep distributions somewhat stabilized may be required to reinvest portions of the income received.

This issue is exacerbated by the fact that ULTY frequently utilizes return of capital distributions. For instance, the latest distribution was classified as 100% return of capital, according to the email notification. Return of capital distributions are a mixed bag because they are considered to be tax-efficient because the income isn't directly taxed. Instead, return of capital distributions reduce an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. So as long as ULTY continues to prioritize ROC, investors can effectively defer taxes for an extended period of time.

However, these ROC distributions also have a direct negative impact on the underlying NAV. So if the fund issues a payout of $0.50, the share price will decline by this amount. If the fund continues to issue these large payouts while seeing minimal upside growth that falls under the distribution amounts, the share price will continue to trend lower. This is why I believe that ULTY's high distribution rate can be harmful unless the market continues to rise without interruption.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on ULTY to a hold after the new strategy shift. While the new strategy includes exposure to less volatile companies, I want to observe how these changes translate to real world performance. I believe there's a chance that the attempt to limit downside erosion may be successful, but I am also cautious about the inclusion of leverage that can also amplify downside risks. While I'll admit that I like the holdings within ULTY better, I am disappointed in the sheer NAV erosion that has occurred throughout the fund's lifetime. This makes it difficult to justify the large distribution rate being paid out and I think that ULTY would be more suitable as a long-term position if the yield was brought down to the 40% range.

data.title: CAVA: Sell This Rebound Ahead Of Q4 Earnings (Downgrade)
data.description:
Without a doubt, the restaurant sector has been one of the most volatile industries in 2025, and in my view, the tumult is likely to continue well into 2026 as restaurants are forced to rethink their business models in an era where low-cost labor rates are skyrocketing and consumer demand for eating out is feeble.

CAVA (CAVA), the Mediterranean bowl chain, is one of the companies that has faced the harshest reckonings. A sharp deceleration in same-store sales growth decimated CAVA's stock after its Q3 earnings print in early November. Still, the stock is regaining momentum as a former Wall Street darling; since bottoming near $44 in November, shares of CAVA have now rallied ~50% to the mid-$60s. Now, the stock is down "only" ~50% over the past year. The question for investors now is: is the bad news already priced in, or is this rally a head fake?

I last wrote a buy article on CAVA in late November, when the stock was trading in the high $40s. At the time, I noted that the selloff likely overlooked CAVA's growing fan base, its innovative and appealing loyalty program, and its substantial white space for new location expansion within the U.S.

All of that was relative to a more modest valuation, however. In the $60s, I see far more risk for CAVA, and I am not keen to take a further bet on this stock especially as it grapples with its core struggle: a weakened consumer economy, and changing consumer perceptions of value when it comes to lunch bowls. Given the sharp rebound rally in CAVA stock (that has now taken the stock above where it was before reporting its disastrous Q3 earnings), I'm downgrading CAVA to a sell rating. To me, the stock is set up for another post-earnings disappointment.

Valuation multiples have reached speculative levels again

Let's get one thing out of the way: I actually think CAVA is a fantastic company, and I'm both a fan of its fundamentals, its growth opportunities, as well as its food (CAVA is now my preferred so-called "slop bowl," overtaking Chipotle (CMG) in my personal taste rankings). At the same time, however, as an investor I have to balance CAVA's fundamental quality against its price: and after its sharp recent rally, I no longer think that equation favors bulls.

At current share prices near $66, CAVA trades at a market cap of $7.25 billion. Netting off the $385.8 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet, which is debt-free, gives us an enterprise value of $6.86 billion.

As a reminder, CAVA recently slashed its full-year FY25 outlook to 3-4% same-store sales growth (a 150bps midpoint reduction versus 4-6% growth expected previously), and $148-$152 million in adjusted EBITDA, which reflects a 12.8% midpoint adjusted EBITDA margin.

At this point in the year, it's more appropriate to focus on forward multiples. Right now, Wall Street is expecting CAVA to hit $1.42 billion in sales in FY26, which reflects 21% y/y growth (the majority of that growth will have to come from net-new store openings if same-store sales growth remains at a weak level).

If we generously assume that CAVA can retain its 12.8% adjusted EBITDA margin next year, adjusted EBITDA would be ~$181.8 million. (Note here: there are plenty of margin headwinds that may make even margin maintenance difficult; in the most recent quarter, CAVA's restaurant margins already fell back -100bps y/y).

This positions CAVA's valuation multiples at 38x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. CAVA stock is now towering back into its usual position: trading at a sharp premium over the majority of the restaurant sector:

We think it's notable that CAVA is now trading at basically a ~2x valuation premium to its fast-casual rivals like Chipotle and Shake Shack (SHAK), despite the fact that CAVA's same-store sales growth is now essentially in line with Shake Shack's.

For CAVA to grow into a more normalized valuation, it basically has to double its profitability scale. With same-store sales growth grinding down to the low single digits, this is looking increasingly difficult, especially as CAVA's location growth plans only call for about ~17% net location growth per annum. CAVA is no longer an appealing rebound play: it's a premium bet.

Q4 look-ahead: many pitfalls ahead

Investors and traders have clambered back into CAVA stock over the past few months, while ignoring a potential time bomb ahead: the company's Q4 earnings release, which is due shortly in early to mid February. In my view, these will be the key watch items to be careful of:

  • Comp sales trends. CAVA's comp sales have been weak over the past two quarters, at 2.1% in Q2 and 1.9% in Q3. No restaurant stocks have reported Q4 results yet, but in the wake of tepid holiday retail sales spending announced in January, and a continued weakness in the U.S. consumer, we're not expecting a significant turnaround.
  • Increasing competitive and promo pressure. We're also noting that restaurants are becoming more aggressive in utilizing promotions to try to win back consumers. Though it doesn't directly compete against CAVA, we note that McDonald's (MCD) has pushed all of its franchisees to advertise a national $5 Meal Deal price point, alongside a slew of regular promotions. Chipotle (CMG) has been running more email marketing campaigns offering freebies like free guac or "double protein on us" to try to get diners back in-store. Outside of its new loyalty program (where many frequent diners will be sure to notice that point redemption values are now higher, despite higher earning rates as well), CAVA largely stays out of the promotion fray. We'll see if this begins to impact comp sales even further.
  • Numerous margin pressures. In CAVA's most recent quarter, the company reported a -100bps y/y decline in restaurant-level operating margins (an industry measure that excludes corporate overhead), owing to inflation in raw food costs plus labor. Labor is a particularly important item for us to watch in 2026. On January 1, many states boosted their tipped and non-tipped minimum wages. New York City, for example, boosted metro minimum wages to $17, from $16.50 (a 3% increase).
  • Potential for a consecrative 2026 outlook. The main item that I think will represent downside risk for CAVA stock is the potential for the company to release a weak 2026 outlook alongside its Q4 earnings. With minimum wage increases stacked on top of weaker consumer data, we think the company will still plan to open an aggressive number of locations in 2026, given its healthy balance sheet (versus ~70 planned store openings in 2025), but we also think the company will guide softly on comp sales and on adjusted EBITDA margins and expansion, which may damage the stock's recent rally.

Key takeaways

All in all, I think the upcoming earnings season is more likely to be a bloodbath than a tailwind for CAVA, especially as it stock has quickly vaulted back up into premium valuation territory. At this juncture, I'm not hesitating to lock in my gains on CAVA and move to the sidelines, especially with cheaper and equally fantastic fast-casual restaurant stocks like Shake Shack (SHAK) trading at much more reasonable prices.

data.title: Greenback Mostly Consolidates, While Yen Gyrations Point To Nervous Market
data.description:
The greenback (DXY) is mostly softer against the G10 currencies today. Initial gains had carried the dollar to almost JPY154.90 before a large order, market nervousness over intervention saw a quick drop in early European turnover through yesterday’s lows (~JPY153.30) to about JPY153.20 before steadying. Dollar sentiment is poor amid fear of intervention and a new Plaza-like agreement. We suspect such talk is widely exaggerated, and the success of verbal intervention means that material intervention is not needed. Technically, the dollar looks stretched, and tomorrow’s conclusion of the FOMC meeting has often been a source of volatility.

President Trump’s threat of 25% tariffs on South Korea, where the parliament has not approved last year’s trade/investment pact, initially weighed on the won and Korean equities. However, the market concluded it was likely rhetoric as the 100% tariff threat on Canada a few days ago, and the tariff threat for those who trade with Iran, and on several European countries for opposing the US acquisition of Greenland.

Prices

G10

• The euro rose for the third consecutive session yesterday and recorded the session high slightly above $1.1905 in the North American morning. It stopped slightly short of last year’s high recorded on September 17 when the Federal Reserve delivered its first rate cut of the year (~$1.1920). It is in the half of a cent range today below $1.1900. It spent most of yesterday above the upper Bollinger Band, which is found near $1.1865 today. The low in North America yesterday was about $1.1835. Position adjusting ahead of tomorrow’s FOM meeting outcome could see this taken out.

• The fear-coordinated intervention drove the dollar to almost JPY153.30 yesterday. After approaching JPY154.90 in late Asia-Pacific turnover today, the dollar inexplicably dropped to the session low near JPY153.20. Some attributed it to a rate check or actual intervention, but we suspect it reflects market nervousness. It quickly recovered to around JPY154.20 before stalling. In fact, the sharp drop, to the dollar’s lowest level since last November, shows verbal intervention to have been effective and reduces the chances of material intervention. BOJ data yesterday showed no evidence of actual material intervention. Tomorrow, Japan auctions 40-year bonds, and the foreign exchange market may be sensitive to the results.

• The dollar’s sell-off in North America saw sterling briefly trade above $1.37 for the first time since last September 17 (Fed Day). It reached almost $1.3715 yesterday. Last September’s high was about $1.3725. It is straddling $1.3700 in late London morning turnover. It has not been much below $1.3665 today. As it did before the weekend, sterling briefly traded more than three standard deviations above the 20-day moving average yesterday. Although settled back below it, sterling remains well above the upper Bollinger Band, set at two standard deviations above the 20-day moving average, found near $1.3660 today. Yesterday’s low was around the $1.3635 area.

• The Canadian dollar reversed early gains and was one of the few G10 currencies that finished lower against the US dollar yesterday. The market seemed to accept that the 100% tariff that the US threatened was like the bluster around the tariff threat over the commercial showing former President Reagan critical of the use of tariffs. The US dollar bottomed in Europe yesterday near CAD1.3670 and reached CAD1.3725 in the North American afternoon. A bullish hammer candlestick may have been forged yesterday. Follow-through greenback buying today initially saw almost CAD1.3740, before selling pressure in early European turnover today pushed it back to almost CAD1.3700. A move above CAD1.3740 could spur corrective upticks in the CAD1.3770-1.3800 area.

• The Australian dollar reached $0.6940 yesterday and stopped 1/100th of a cent from the September 2024 highs. There is not much on the charts now until the psychological $0.7000 level. However, the 2.5-cent rally in six sessions has stretched it. It frayed the three-standard deviation (from the 20-day moving average) mark and settled above the upper Bollinger Band for the fourth consecutive session. It is consolidating so far today, holding above $0.6900, and recorded a session high in late European morning activity slightly above $0.6930.

EM

• The dollar was sold below MXN17.25 near midday in NY yesterday before recovering back toward the session high, a little below MXN17.40. The greenback settled slightly higher on the session, for only the second time in 11 sessions. It is trading quietly between roughly MXN17.3165 and MXN17.3640 today. The five-day moving average is slightly above MXN17.40, and the dollar has not settled above it for more than two weeks.

• The dollar recorded a new low against the offshore yuan yesterday near CNH6.9440 and is consolidating today. It hardly traded above CNH6.9565. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate at CNY6.9858 (CNY6.9843 yesterday), the first higher fix in three sessions.

• Indian markets re-opened from yesterday’s holiday, and news of a trade agreement with the EU and a softer dollar since last Friday saw the rupee consolidate. The US dollar set a record against the rupee at the end of last week near INR91.97. It is trading between INR91.6535 and INR19.8950 today.

Other Markets

• Global equities are higher. The MSCI Asia Pacific Index reached a record high today as most of the bourses were higher, though China was narrowly mixed. After a weak start on the back of US threats of 25% tariff on South Korean imports due to the slow-walking of approval of the trade deal, the Kospi (KOSPI) recovered and settled 2.7% higher, as many suspect bluster. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) recovered from early weakness yesterday and settled 0.2% higher. It is up about 0.4% today. The S&P (SPX) and Nasdaq (NDX) futures are 0.25-0.60% higher, respectively, but the Dow (DJI) futures are off about 0.2%.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are firm today. In Japan, the 10-year yield rose five basis points, and the longer-term bond yields are 3-5 bp higher. European yields are slightly higher, as is the 10-year US Treasury (US10Y) near 4.22%. The US sells $70 bln of five-year notes today and $90 bln of six-week bills.

• Gold remains firm, though below yesterday’s $5111 record. It has held mostly above $5014 today. Silver is similar. It is holding below yesterday’s record (~$117.70) and held above $103.

• March WTI found support earlier today near $60 and traded slightly above $61 in Europe.

Data

• With FOMC meeting concluding tomorrow and another government shutdown possible at the end of the week, today’s US data seems of marginal importance. The weekly ADP estimate might receive some passing notice, while the early estimates for next week’s January nonfarm payrolls are around 80k. House prices, the Richmond and Dallas Fed surveys, which barely elicit a response in the best of times, are due. The University of Michigan already reported stronger US consumer confidence, and the Conference Board’s measure is expected to show the same.

• Mexico reports December trade figures today. It is the last important data point before the first estimate of Q4 GDP is released at the end of the week. Despite the disruption emanating from the US, Mexico’s trade deficit fell sharply in the first 11 months of 2025 (~-$1.66 bln vs ~-$20.40 bln in the first 11 months of 2024). Exports rose by about 6.7%, while imports rose by about half as much. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 0.6% quarter-over-quarter expansion in Q4 after a 0.3% contraction in Q3.

• Australia reports December and Q4 CPI first thing tomorrow. December inflation is expected to have accelerated to 3.6% from 3.4% in November. It peaked at 3.8% in October. Officials put more weight on the quarterly reading. Australia’s CPI is seen rising 0.6% in the quarter for a 3.6% year-over-year pace (3.2% in Q3). The underlying measures (trimmed mean and weighted median) are also expected to have risen. The recent string of firm data and hawkish comments by the central bank’s leadership has spurred speculation of a hike as early as next week. The futures market is implying about a 56% chance.

• While China may be the factory of the world, profitability has been a challenge. Still, the campaign against “involution” appears to be yielding results. Chinese industrial companies reported their first annual increase in profits since 2021. Profits rose 5.3% in December, year-over-year, rising for the first time in three months and recovering from a 13%+ drop in November. For the full year, profits rose 0.6%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Credo Technology: Golden Opportunity To Buy The Dip
data.description:
Reasons to continue buying CRDO

Credo Technology's (CRDO) share price has been a real roller-coaster since I initiated coverage of this stock with a strong buy rating. Price at my previous publication was $157.35 and it has rallied closer to $190 two times since then. However, due to its high volatility, it also saw quite sharp price declines, and it is currently at $128. A decline from circa $190 to below $130 means that CRDO has lost approximately one-third of its value, and this share price movement looks more like a short-term volatility rather than something structural.

From the 14-day RSI perspective, it appears that the selloff might have gone too far as CRDO is rapidly approaching the oversold territory as the indicator is already below 40. Meanwhile, such a significant selloff does not look fair from the fundamental perspective as there were only upward earnings revisions over the last few months. As we can see, there were significant EPS forecast upgrades over the last three months, all above 30% for several next quarters. Meanwhile, over the same period CRDO has lost approximately one-third of its share price value. And the positive trend in earnings revisions shows no signs of weakness as 1-month trend also suggests all-positive revisions.

And I believe that all these positive revisions were not only based on CRDO's solid earnings surprise record but supported by various bullish catalysts. Credo is one of the beneficiaries of the current global AI infrastructure buildout as it supplies critical components for connectivity and data centers. Based on the company's revenue and EBITDA growth charts, we can conclude that CRDO is extremely strong in converting soaring demand from data centers into exponential topline and operating profitability growth. Such a strong revenue growth - TTM revenue is higher than FY2024 sales by the factor of 4 - isn't only about financial success, but it significantly boosts credibility and validation for a young company like Credo. Increasing demand for the company's products means that they provide superior quality for highly demanding AI workloads.

Besides rapidly growing revenue, we recently saw another solid validation proof from Credo. The recently signed patent license agreement between Credo and 3M (MMM) is a big milestone for the company, in my opinion. MMM is not jut a company but a true industrial powerhouse of the U.S. and has been one of the pillars of the U.S. economy before the digital revolution. And this legendary company that dominates industrial space is licensing Credo's active electrical cable (AEC) patents. MMM has several industrial contracts (including defense and national security) with the U.S. government, meaning that this company certainly cares about quality and reliability of the technologies they rely on. Therefore, I believe that this licensing agreement is a huge validation victory for CRDO.

Another big piece of evidence supporting my assertion about Credo's fundamental strength is the fact that its EBITDA growth mirrors revenue, which is an indication of healthy unit economics. Operating profitability inflection is quite far in the rearview mirror, and Credo's operating margin has more room for improvement over the long term because the company still invests in R&D aggressively. Among the recent new product releases I want to spotlight CRDO's new memory fanout gearbox for scalable, high-bandwidth AI Inference. This looks like an extremely timely move because the largest memory providers for AI workloads like Micron Technology (MU) and Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) are both citing that the industry faces soaring demand for memory chips from AI data centers. Therefore, memory remains a critical bottleneck for the global AI infrastructure buildout, meaning that vendors are able to capitalize on supply shortage and exert strong pricing power. It is also vital to acknowledge that Credo's new memory product is not competing with giants like MU and SSNLF but complements them by providing connectivity between memory chips and accelerators. With all that being said, CRDO has robust potential to capitalize on the current situation with memory being a bottleneck for AI revolution. Moreover, the company's balance sheet with $800+ million in cash and just $16 million in total debt means that CRDO is well-equipped to continue investing aggressively in R&D.

Therefore, we see that fundamentals remain extremely strong and all CRDO needs to see a pivot in the share price dynamic is a robust headline that can improve the sentiment. This week appears to be the perfect opportunity to buy CRDO before this pivot as two of the four largest AI infrastructure investors are reporting their quarterly earnings. As we all know, the most aggressive data center spenders are Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Meta. Microsoft and Meta are both reporting this week, and there are indications that they will reiterate their aggressive plans to increase AI capex in 2026. On January 9, Meta announced nuclear energy projects totaling up to 6.6 GW to power its aggressive AI infrastructure buildout. It is a massive energy capacity given the fact that the capacity of Meta's huge data center project in Louisiana will deliver approximately 2 GW of compute capacity. Therefore, Meta likely plans to announce new large AI infrastructure projects in 2026. And we are likely to hear bullish thoughts from Microsoft's management during their upcoming earnings call as the company recently won approval for 15 additional data centers in Mount Pleasant, Wisconsin.

Reasons to avoid CRDO

Credo's valuation is aggressively growth-oriented, which is evident from its multiples. As the below board suggests, the only valuation ratio that looks good compared to the sector is CRDO's forward non-GAAP PEG ratio of 0.70, which is lower than the sector by more than two times. At the same time, all other ratios are sky-high compared to peers. It is a classical setup for an aggressive growth story.

While CRDO looks fundamentally strong and the management is working hard on fortifying its potential to capitalize on the accelerating AI infrastructure buildout, growth-oriented multiples mean that the stock is vulnerable to substantial volatility. The global AI landscape is evolving rapidly, and it is affected not only by technological disruptions but also by geopolitics, which makes long-term forecasting extremely complicated. Trade wars and potential commodities market disruptions due to export/import restrictions might significantly affect long-term AI capex plans of the largest tech companies. Therefore, investors should keep in mind that buying aggressive growth stories is inherently risky and the stock might be punished severely if actual revenue and EPS growth is weaker than current forecasts.

Besides potential geopolitical disruptions, let's not forget that there is a significant competition risk. Much larger players like Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL) are also competing in the data center connectivity industry, and these companies are both fundamentally strong and boast much larger financial resources than Credo.

The verdict

Additional large data center projects that might be announced by giants like Meta and Microsoft this week will be a big bullish development for CRDO because the company has a proven record of being one of the main AI infrastructure buildout beneficiaries. Considering this fact, I think that CRDO certainly merits strong buy rating reiteration.

data.title: Am Imminent Shutdown Could Lead To Political Dysfunction, Along The 'Big Cycle' Thesis
data.description:
Another shutdown is likely imminent

After the longest U.S. government shutdown in history just ended in November 2025, another shutdown seems to be imminent, if the government funding deal is not reached by January 30th.

The 2025 shutdown was mostly about healthcare, and it ended with a short-term compromise or a Continuing Resolution CR expiring on January 30th, 2026. Some moderate Democrats joined the Republicans passing the CR, right before the Christmas holidays, but this move was largely criticized by other, more liberal, Democrats.

Subsequently, the House passed the government funding bill and forwarded it to the Senate, where the approval was viewed a formality.

Then, over the weekend the ICE agents killed another U.S. citizen in Minnesota, and the situation is further escalating with anti-ICE protests. Now, the Democrats in Senate want to block the funding bill, and the shutdown seems to be almost certain.

Specifically, the Senate needs to pass six remanding bills, and one of them is funding for the Department of Homeland Security, which includes ICE and Border Patrol.

It appears that the Democrats in the Senate are not likely to pass the funding for ICE and Border Security, given the currently escalating situation in Minnesota, which could spread nationally.

It also appears that the Republicans are unlikely to compromise on this key part of President Trump's agenda, since President Trump feels that he has a strong mandate for his immigration reforms based on the 2024 election results.

Thus, the shutdown is almost certain, which is already reflected in the prediction markets.

Should investors be worried?

The 2025 shutdown did not affect the financial markets, despite being the longest shutdown in history. The stock market actually soared higher during the shutdown - and the main reason is that the bond market remained stable. In fact, the bond market was almost frozen, possibly due to the fact that the economic data was unavailable during the shutdown. Low bond market volatility was positive for the broader financial markets.

The 2026 shutdown could be even longer. First, there will be no desire to end the shutdown due to Christmas like in 2025. But, most importantly, the compromise will be to be more difficult to reach this time, as previously discussed.

Practically, this could mean: 1) another round of furlough government workers with second-round effects on all government contractors, and 2) another extended period of economic data blackout.

Generally, government shutdowns don't affect stock markets because eventually the economic contraction during the shutdown is reversed once the government reopens.

The major risk is to the bond market if the shutdown leads to downgrade of Treasury Bonds due to the governance issue. Note, even with the shutdown, the interest payments on Treasury bonds are guaranteed as this is not related to the debt ceiling issue.

Thus, generally, investors should overlook the risks associated with the potential shutdown.

Will it lead to political dysfunction?

However, this situation is more complex when viewed within the macro context. The analysis of the current situation is appropriate from the Ray Dalio's Big Cycle theory.

Ray Dalio wrote a book How Countries Go Broke: The Big Cycle with the following sequence:

  1. Peak Prosperity
  2. Over‑indebtedness
  3. Wealth gaps widen
  4. Internal conflict rises
  5. Political dysfunction emerges
  6. Loss of reserve currency influence
  7. External rivals rise
  8. Disorderly restructuring.

Within Dalio's 'big cycle' sequence, the U.S. rose to power after WWII, which led to the period of peace and prosperity, with the dominance of U.S.-led institutions, and the U.S. dollar as the global reserve currency. This peace and prosperity phase likely peaked in early 2000s.

During the next phase the U.S. debt levels started to increase with the series of crisis, the 2000 dot-com burst, the 2008 great financial crises, and the 2000 covid crisis. This is when asset bubbles begin forming, wealth inequality begins widening, political conflicts emerged.

The internal conflicts could lead to political dysfunction, which could lead to the loss of reserve currency status - and this is the ultimate risk with international implications.

Thus, within the Dalio's "big cycle" theory, the likely shutdown could pose a major risk if it leads to political dysfunction.

A political dysfunction will be reflected as the inability to compromise - and this will be due to the deep ideological differences. If the Democrats refuse to fund ICE, and the Republicans refuse to compromise, and the current peaceful protest turn violent, and spread nationwide, it could signal what Dalio calls "disorderly restructuring."

Implications for investors

It's never easy to predict what will happen. Will the Administration compromise once the risks outweigh the benefits? I think this is likely.

Will the Democrats once again cave-in without the compromise? I think this is less likely in the current situation. In my opinion, there will be a compromise to end the shutdown.

Thus, the likely shutdown might contribute to some volatility, in addition to other events, such as geopolitical, and AI bubble, and the Fed independence. But overall, the likely shutdown will not affect the markets significantly by itself.

Obviously, if there is no compromise and the situation escalates along the Dalio's cycle, the risk is a sharply falling U.S. Dollar (DXY), sharply rising 10Y yield (US10Y), and a major correction to the stock market (SP500).

The problem is that other events also have the same negative effects. The geopolitical events are promoting the Sell America trade (sell USD and U.S. Treasury Bonds), while the Fed independence issue is risking runaway inflation also with higher yields and collapsing U.S. Dollar.

The likely shutdown seems to be part of the broader policy mix that carry the risk of higher interest rates and falling U.S. Dollar, and thus, it's hard to differentiate to what event the markets are reacting.

The bond market volatility has been very low, and none of these risks are reflected here. The forex market has also been stable, so none of these risks are reflected here either. The stock market is near the all-time highs, ignoring all the risks.

The only assets pricing in Dalio's big cycle outcome are the precious metals, gold (GLD) and silver (SLV). So, this is a warning sign.

data.title: REGENXBIO: Key FDA Decision On Hunter Syndrome Just Weeks Away
data.description:
Thesis

Recently, we got an update from Regenxbio Inc. (RGNX). Management highlighted 2026 as a make-or-break year for the company, and I’m inclined to agree. If you look at it, there are quite a few near-term regulatory and clinical catalysts that could help move this company from a development-stage biotech into more of a commercial gene therapy company, which is what investors want to see this year. Now, the most important update we got has to be RGX-202 for Duchenne muscular dystrophy, where we saw the new 18-month data from the pivotal dose showing patients outperforming the expected disease decline by a pretty wide margin. We will get into this in more detail later, but for now, I think it strengthens the overall case for the mid-2026 accelerated BLA submission they have planned. We also have some pivotal topline data expected sometime in early 2Q26, with the confirmatory enrollment tipped to be largely complete. The big spend for Regenxbio has to be on regulatory readiness rather than just speculative hope.

Elsewhere, the company is just weeks away from an FDA decision on RGX-121 for Hunter syndrome, with a PDUFA date set on February 8th next month. So I thought it would be a good time to assess where the company is at ahead of this decision. The outcome will have a significant impact on the stock in either direction. In my previous coverage, back in 2025, I tipped for the next big breakthrough for Regenxbio to be RGX-121, with the stock most likely gaining momentum leading into the decision date. Since then, shares are up about 45%. However, it's important to note that RGX-121 doesn’t make up the entire portfolio and that the company has multiple strong candidates in various stages of progression.

RGX-121

Now, RGX-121/clemidsogene lanparvovec could, of course, generate both some future revenue and a valuable Priority Review Voucher, which would add financial optionality. Essentially, I'm tipping for the Priority Review Voucher to give them a major financial/strategic boost going forward. It should allow either a faster FDA review of another key product or, if they desired, they could sell it for significant non-dilutive cash, somewhere in the nine-figure range. Now, they may actually need this capital later on for a potential commercial launch if things go well in early February.

In terms of RGX-121’s potential, as you know, it's an investigational, one-time gene therapy developed to treat Mucopolysaccharidosis Type II (MPS II) or Hunter syndrome. Now, this is a rare, X-linked lysosomal storage disorder that most commonly affects boys. It’s caused by a deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme iduronate-2-sulfatase (I2S), which we normally see break down long-chain sugar molecules called glycosaminoglycans. So without enough levels of I2S, GAGs will inevitably accumulate in cells and tissues, particularly in the central nervous system, which, as you can imagine, will lead to multiple issues. We often see progressive neurocognitive decline and developmental delay by about the 18- to 24-month range. Now, in terms of current therapies, there are multiple intravenous enzyme replacement therapies on the market; however, they don’t actually reliably reach the brain. And that’s because they struggle to cross the blood-brain barrier, which leaves the severe neurological manifestations, for the most part, untreated. So what makes RGX-121 a bit unique is that it’s designed to address that unmet need by attempting to deliver a functional IDS gene directly into the CNS. Now, this is a bit complicated, and Regenxbio's strategy is to use an AAV9 viral vector, which, in theory, should enable cells to produce normal I2S enzymes long-term and also give some kind of widespread cross-correction in neighboring cells that also need that enzyme.

The reason I mention the mechanism in such detail is that I believe it's quite important when evaluating the whole package the FDA is currently reviewing. Now, the most pivotal clinical evidence comes from the Phase I/II/III CAMPSIITE trial. That was a global, open-label, multi-cohort study that enrolled boys from age 4 months to 5 years with neuronopathic MPS II. The trial included dose-finding and pivotal phases. Initial cohorts at much lower dose levels showed that RGX-121 was pretty well-tolerated with no drug-related serious adverse events. It also demonstrated biomarker and clinical signals of efficacy. So that would be reductions in cerebrospinal fluid GAGs, including heparan sulfate, which is one of the main GAGs that we see build up and can actually leave behind toxic fragments when it is only partially degraded. There were also reductions of a key fragment called HS D2S6, which is chemically the same as a piece of heparan sulfate, but HS D2S6 is important in that it strongly correlates with disease severity in the brain. We often see high levels of this in the cerebrospinal fluid when there is ongoing lysosomal dysfunction and neurodegeneration. So I think this will be good viewing for the FDA. However, the FDA won’t want to just see the removal of GAGs in the fluid, but they will also want to see measurable I2S enzyme. In the trial, I2S had been undetectable before treatment, but post RGX-121, there were measurable levels. Patients also showed some sustained neurodevelopmental skill acquisition and all-around daily activity improvements. Impressively, some were able to discontinue or avoid ERT entirely after treatment, which was nice to see.

Now, coming to the most pivotal phase of CAMPSIITE, the trial met its primary endpoint with statistical significance. Those treated with RGX-121 showed a median 86% reduction in HS D2S6 at 16 weeks, approaching normal levels. It was a very important biomarker for the application and one that is strongly correlated with disease burden and, more importantly, the neurocognitive outcomes the FDA wants to see. Because of the improvement in neurocognitive activity, I think this is why it was used as a reasonable surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval. Elsewhere, the follow-up data demonstrated decent sustained reductions in HS D2S6, often above 80%, which was seen 1 to 2 years post-treatment. Now, patients at the pivotal dose also exhibited continued neurodevelopmental stability or gain versus the natural history of MPS II. With many patients being ERT-free at the last follow-up, this suggests to me that this could be a marketing point of the therapy if we see an improvement in that the treatment could replace/reduce the need for regular infusions. It's also worth noting that, in general, there have been no major safety concerns raised by the FDA during review.

A Reminder of the TAM

I went into the details of the potential market for RGX-121 in my previous coverage, but as a quick reminder, as a one-time, potentially curative treatment, we could see a premium price if approved. That would place the price tag in an estimated range of $1 to $2 million per patient. And since it may actually treat the neurological component of the disease, unlike most of the current lifelong enzyme replacement therapies, I think we could see it capture a large share of eligible patients. But as you know, since it is an ultra-rare genetic disorder that affects just roughly 1 in 100,000 to 170,000 births, eligible patients will be quite limited.

Elsewhere in the Pipeline

Regenxbio also partnered with AbbVie on the wet AMD program to see potential benefit from it in 2026. I think we could see ABBV-RGX-314 position them for exposure to a pretty massive non-rare disease market. The Phase III data is expected sometime in late 2026, which also comes with a $100 million milestone payment, likely in the first half of the year.

We have to consider it as an underappreciated source of upside for Regenxbio. There’s the benefit of both some near-term cash and long-term commercial leverage. Keep in mind that in the U.S., ABBV-RGX-314 profits and losses are shared equally among the two companies. It should give them meaningful exposure to what may become the first gene therapy for wet AMD. Also outside the U.S., the company is still entitled to royalties and milestone payments. The most notable of which would be the amended diabetic retinopathy development plan. It includes that $100 million upon the first patient dosed in the Phase IIb/III trial, and there is also an additional $100 million upon the first patient dosed in the subsequent Phase III. Now, this could possibly create some potential non-dilutive funding as early as 2026. There would also be a lot of upside to a very large ophthalmology market.

RGX-202

On that latest update, the company reported some pretty positive 18-month functional data from their Phase I/II AFFINITY DUCHENNE trial. Results were all around quite decent, with all patients outperforming expected disease progression on the North Star Ambulatory Assessment, as you can see below in the readout. So, based on the cTAP model, it shows on average a 7.4-point improvement versus the predicted decline. This was also consistent with the average 6.6-point benefit they saw at 12 months. Now, we should expect the same pivotal topline data from the 30-patient trial sometime in early 2Q26 and hopefully after a plan to submit a BLA under the accelerated approval pathway later this year. It's just another big upcoming catalyst for the stock, whilst investors should keep a lookout for enrollment in the confirmatory study. This will be another step in the right direction and help them get ready for FDA/EMA interactions in the first half of the year.

Valuation

If we look at Regnexbio from a valuation standpoint, shares also look quite compelling relative to the stage and upcoming catalysts this year. There’s a lot that could serve as potential upward stock rerates, and some investors may view current levels as a good opportunity to buy in. The stock is now trading at an enterprise value of roughly $664 million, which I feel is pretty modest for a company with multiple late-stage gene therapy assets and also two potential near-term regulatory events. Now, on a revenue multiple basis, Regenxbio trades at about 3.3x forward EV to sales, which is a decent discount to the healthcare sector median, which sits around 3.8x. This tells me that the market isn't quite fully pricing in the potential commercial launches. Importantly, however, the company also holds about $274 million in cash, which nearly offsets the $268 million they have in debt. This should give them some balance sheet flexibility heading into the readouts and an FDA decision.

As of the last earnings, the current cash and cash equivalents figure was about $302 million. And based on a prior 9-month operational spend of $234.7 million so far in 2025, that would give us a monthly spend of about $27 million. It leaves them with a projected cash runway of just under a year at current rates. The stock also trades with an average 10-day volume of about 800,000 shares, which is pretty healthy liquidity for a biotech now more focused on late‑stage therapy catalysts.

Risks

Now, since the company is potentially weeks away from an FDA decision and also the readout for Duchenne is expected in 2Q26, the current valuation is clearly showing some skepticism right now. On one hand, it makes the stock look attractive if even one of the major programs converts into a commercial asset, but on the other hand, there are a lot of risks. The stock has been showing good upward momentum until now and is up about 75% on the 1-year chart.

Now, there are clearly a few specific things the FDA could push back on in RGX-121’s application, despite its overall strength. I expect these to be related to evidence strength and long-term certainty. First, the approval case is going to rely heavily on the reduction of HS D2S6 in the cerebrospinal fluid as a surrogate endpoint, not the direct randomized cognitive outcomes. So there is always the aspect of the FDA questioning among themselves how well that biomarker truly predicts long-term neurodevelopmental benefit. Second, the pivotal dataset is based on a pretty small, open-label population compared to natural history. Which can raise concerns about bias and variability in neurodevelopmental assessments, roughly 13 patients in an open-case setting.

Looking Ahead

So, looking forward to the next big milestone will, of course, be the FDA PDUFA decision for RGX‑121 on February 8th. After that, there is the pivotal RGX‑202 Duchenne data expected sometime in early 2Q26 and progress in the AbbVie retinal programs to watch out for. That also includes diabetic retinopathy dosing milestones. Additionally, we can expect 4Q25 earnings to come sometime in mid-March 2026, which should give us an update on cash burn and, if the FDA outcome goes well, a very positive and bullish outlook on 2026.
